question: what are fermented vegetables called
title: What Are Fermented Vegetables Called? The Names, the Process, and Why It Matters
metaDescription: Lacto-fermented vegetables go by many names — kimchi, sauerkraut, curtido, tsukemono. Here's what they all have in common, how the process works, and why these living foods are so different from pickles.
content:
# What Are Fermented Vegetables Called? The Names, the Process, and Why It Matters

When people first hear me talk about fermented vegetables at the market, sometimes they've never put words to what they're experiencing. They know about pickles. They might know about sauerkraut. But they don't necessarily know there's a whole family of foods built on the same biological principle — vegetables transformed by the same ancient process that humans figured out long before refrigeration, long before food science, long before anyone knew what a bacterium was.

So let's name them, understand what connects them, and talk about why "fermented vegetable" is not just a trend but a description of one of the oldest food technologies in human history.

## The Family Name: Lacto-Fermented Vegetables

The technical name is lacto-fermented vegetables, or sometimes cultured vegetables. "Lacto" doesn't mean dairy — it refers to lactic acid bacteria, the family of microorganisms responsible for this type of fermentation. Lactic acid bacteria (LAB) are naturally present on raw vegetables. When you create the right conditions — anaerobic, meaning without oxygen, and with enough salt to give the LAB an advantage over spoilage organisms — those bacteria multiply and ferment the vegetable carbohydrates into lactic acid.

That lactic acid is what preserves the food. It drops the pH, creating an acidic environment that's hostile to harmful bacteria. It also creates the sour, tangy flavor profile that makes fermented vegetables distinctive. And as long as you don't heat the finished product, the live bacteria responsible remain active and viable — that's what makes these foods probiotic.

Research published in the *NCBI Bookshelf* on lactic acid fermentations describes the process plainly: the raw vegetable is shredded, salt is added at approximately 2 percent by weight, the salt draws out moisture from the vegetable to create a brine, and anaerobic conditions are maintained to prevent spoilage. The rest is biology.

## The Names From Around the World

Every major food culture developed its own version of lacto-fermented vegetables. Same process, different ingredients, different flavors, different names.

**Sauerkraut** — German for "sour cabbage" — is the version most North Americans know first. Shredded cabbage, salt, time. Simple. Tart. Crispy when it's young, softer and more complex as it ages. It's the fermented vegetable that most people are at least a little familiar with, even if they've only had the pasteurized supermarket version.

**Kimchi** is the Korean tradition, and it's a little more elaborate — napa cabbage or daikon radish, gochugaru (Korean red pepper flakes), garlic, ginger, fish sauce or salted shrimp, and sometimes other vegetables. Hundreds of varieties exist. The bacterial communities that develop in kimchi are different from sauerkraut's, shaped by those additional ingredients, but the underlying mechanism is the same.

**Curtido** is a Central American fermented cabbage relish — lighter fermentation, typically with oregano and lime, eaten alongside pupusas and other dishes. **Tsukemono** is the Japanese tradition of pickled vegetables, some of which are lacto-fermented, others salt-pressed or marinated in vinegar. **Kvass** in Eastern Europe is traditionally made by fermenting bread or vegetables — beet kvass is a classic.

Research published in PMC on the microbial communities of 75 homemade fermented vegetables found remarkable diversity across traditions — different vegetables, different LAB strains, different flavor profiles — but a consistent underlying process of lactic acid fermentation driving them all.

## Fermented Vegetables vs. Pickles: The Crucial Distinction

This is the part that trips people up, and it's important enough to spend some real time on.

Pickles — the commercial pickles you find in most grocery stores — are not fermented. They're cucumbers (or other vegetables) submerged in a vinegar brine. The vinegar creates an acidic environment that prevents spoilage, and the cucumbers absorb that sour flavor. There are no live bacteria in commercial vinegar pickles. They're preserved by acid, not by fermentation.

Traditional dill pickles — the kind made the old way, in a salt brine, left at room temperature for a few weeks — are fermented. They're lacto-fermented cucumbers. The bacteria that naturally live on the cucumber's skin go to work in that salt brine, producing lactic acid, creating sourness, and generating billions of live bacteria.

The rule is simple: if it's made with vinegar, it's not a probiotic food. If it's made with salt and time, it is. Read the label. A genuine lacto-fermented pickle will say "raw," "naturally fermented," or "no vinegar added." It will be refrigerated. The brine will be slightly cloudy.

UC Davis's fermentation research program — one of the most active in the country — has documented this distinction thoroughly. Fermented facts from their program confirm that lacto-fermented vegetables and vinegar pickles are fundamentally different products despite the similarity in appearance and flavor.

## Why Raw Vegetables Are Already Prepared for Fermentation

Here's something that blew my mind when I first really thought about it. The bacteria responsible for lacto-fermentation — the *Lactobacillus*, *Leuconostoc*, and *Pediococcus* species that do the work — are already on the surface of your vegetables. Every unwashed, fresh cabbage leaf. Every cucumber straight from the garden. Every carrot, radish, turnip.

These bacteria are part of the vegetable's own life web. They're living on the plant the same way the soil bacteria live in the ground — as part of a complex ecosystem that the plant participates in. When you wash your vegetables, you wash some of them off. When you cook your vegetables, you kill them all. But when you ferment them, you invite them to transform the food in a way that benefits you.

This is a little bit of what Albert Howard meant when he wrote about the web of life that connects plants to soil to people. It's not just the soil that's alive. The food itself carries life — and traditional fermentation methods, developed over thousands of years, were ways of honoring and extending that life.

## The Diversity of What You Can Ferment

Almost any vegetable can be lacto-fermented. Cabbage is the classic because its cell structure and natural bacterial population are particularly well-suited to the process. But the tradition is far broader.

Research on fermented vegetable diversity documents successful fermentation of cucumbers, carrots, beets, turnips, cauliflower, green beans, garlic, onions, peppers, and dozens of others. Mix-vegetable ferments are common — kimchi often contains radish alongside the cabbage; mixed pickle crocks have been a fixture of household food preservation across cultures for centuries.

The salt percentage, the temperature, and the duration all affect the outcome. A 2% brine at room temperature produces a mild, quickly fermented product in five to seven days. A 3% brine fermented cool and slow over several weeks produces something more complex, more sour, more deeply flavored — and potentially with a more diverse bacterial community.

Experimenting is part of the tradition. Different vegetables, different salt levels, different herbs and spices — every variation produces something new. That variety is not just culinary fun. It also means your gut is being exposed to a wider range of bacterial strains and bioactive compounds.

Y'all, these foods aren't a trend. They're a tradition. The terminology is new but the biology is ancient, and your body was built to recognize it.

---

scottyQuotes: The bacteria responsible for fermentation are already on your vegetables. Every fresh cabbage leaf, every cucumber from the garden. You're not adding life — you're inviting the life that's already there to do its work.
If it's made with vinegar, it's not a probiotic food. If it's made with salt and time, it is. Read the label.
These foods aren't a trend. They're a tradition. The terminology is new but the biology is ancient, and your body was built to recognize it.
